works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: "Jiankang Shilu" is a collection of historical materials from the Six Dynasties compiled by Xu Song in the Tang Dynasty. Volume 4 of this book mentions that "The Emperor of Wu took the throne from Renyin in the first year of Huangwu to Bingshen in the first year of Zhide in the Tang Dynasty, a total of 535 years." It is known that this book was written no earlier than the era of Suzong of the Tang Dynasty, and the book records the establishment of the capital. A separate history of historical facts and anecdotes of the Wu, Eastern Jin, Song, Qi, Liang, and Chen dynasties in Jiankang (now Nanjing). There are 20 volumes in the book, which are incomplete. The author said in the "Preface" that this book "reflects the actions of the kings and ministers of the Six Dynasties. Things are detailed and concise, and the text is essential, and it is not necessary to mention it. The land, mountains, rivers, cities, palaces and gardens were established at that time, or they prospered and destroyed each other. The location is used to preserve the historical relics. If there are strange things that have been heard separately, and the words do not belong to each other, they are all noted to facilitate the understanding. It makes it possible to browse around without being bored, and to make appointments without missing anything." It was written in "Three Kingdoms" , "Book of Jin", "Book of Song", "Book of Nan Qi", "Book of Liang", "Book of Chen", "Shui Jing Zhu" and "Southern History", and records some information that is not included in the official history, so It is often cited by those who later researched the historical events of the Six Dynasties.

works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: The concept of "Taicheng" focuses on the symbol of imperial power in the ancient capital, where the old palaces and gardens were located. The poet uses the image of the Tang Dynasty that "thousands of households and thousands of doors have become weeds" to question and hold the author accountable for the work "Flowers in the Backyard of the Yushu" written by Empress Chen, who lived in drunken dreams and died. The historical facts of debauchery and debauchery that harmed the country can be understood at a glance, and they are as clear as a fire.

works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587: 台城六代竞豪华，结绮临春事最奢。万户千门成野草，只缘一曲后庭花。
晚云阴映下空城，六代累累夕照明。玉井已干龙不起，金瓯虽破虎曾争。亦知霸世才难得，却是蒙尘事最平。深谷作陵山作海，茂弘流辈莫伤情。

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: This poem was written when Tianbao was traveling to Jinling. This poem praises the past and laments the present, lamenting that although the situation in Jinling is like a tiger on a dragon's side, the emperors of the past dynasties have rotated around like a revolving lantern. It can be seen that the strength of a country lies in human affairs rather than natural dangers. This poem seems to be a warning to the people in power not to imitate Empress Chen’s debauchery and pursue the country.
works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587: 石头巉岩如虎踞，凌波欲过沧江去。钟山龙盘走势来，秀色横分历阳树。四十馀帝三百秋，功名事迹随东流。白马金鞍谁家子，吹唇虎啸凤凰楼。金陵昔时何壮哉！席卷英豪天下来。冠盖散为烟雾尽，金舆玉座成寒灰。扣剑悲吟空咄嗟，梁陈白骨乱如麻。天子龙沉景阳井，谁歌玉树后庭花。此地伤心不能道，目下离离长春草。送尔长江万里心，他年来访南山老。

works.\u4f5c\u54c1\u540d: 《登梅岗望金陵，赠族侄高座寺僧中孚》
works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587: 钟山抱金陵，霸气昔腾发。天开帝王居，海色照宫阙。群峰如逐鹿，奔走相驰突。江水九道来，云端遥明没。时迁大运去，龙虎势休歇。我来属天清，登览穷楚越。吾宗道门秀，特异鸾凤骨。众星罗青天，明者独有月。冥居顺生理，草木不剪伐。烟窗引蔷薇，石壁老野蕨。吴风谢安屐，白足傲履袜。几宿一下山，萧然忘干谒。谈经演金偈，降鹤舞海雪。时闻天香来，了与世事绝。佳游不可得，春风惜远别。赋诗留岩屏，千载庶不灭。

works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587: 山围故国周遭在，潮打空城寂寞回。淮水东边旧时月，夜深还过女墙来。

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: The author was born in the late Tang Dynasty, had a bumpy official career, and witnessed the difficult state affairs, so he used the ancient times to describe the present, and sighed for the late Tang Dynasty.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: It describes the poet's climb up to the old stone city in late spring and looked far into the distance, expressing his nostalgia for the past and sadness for the present.
works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587: 万里伤心极目春，东南王气只逡巡。野花相笑落满地，山鸟自惊啼傍人。谩道城池须险阻，可知豪杰亦埃尘。太平寺主惟轻薄，却把三公与贼臣。

白云红树路纡萦，古殿长廊次第行。
南望水连桃叶渡，北来山枕石头城。
一尘不到心源净，万有俱空眼界清。
竹院逢僧旧曾识，旋披禅衲为相迎。

works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: "Jiankang Shilu" is a collection of historical materials from the Six Dynasties compiled by Xu Song in the Tang Dynasty. Volume 4 of this book mentions that "The Emperor of Wu took the throne from Renyin in the first year of Huangwu to Bingshen in the first year of Zhide in the Tang Dynasty, a total of 535 years." It is known that this book was written no earlier than the era of Suzong of the Tang Dynasty, and the book records the establishment of the capital. A separate history of historical facts and anecdotes of the Wu, Eastern Jin, Song, Qi, Liang, and Chen dynasties in Jiankang (now Nanjing). There are 20 volumes in the book, which are incomplete. The author said in the "Preface" that this book "reflects the actions of the kings and ministers of the Six Dynasties. Things are detailed and concise, and the text is essential, and it is not necessary to mention it. The land, mountains, rivers, cities, palaces and gardens were established at that time, or they prospered and destroyed each other. The location is used to preserve the historical relics. If there are strange things that have been heard separately, and the words do not belong to each other, they are all noted to facilitate the understanding. It makes it possible to browse around without being bored, and to make appointments without missing anything." It was written in "Three Kingdoms" , "Book of Jin", "Book of Song", "Book of Nan Qi", "Book of Liang", "Book of Chen", "Shui Jing Zhu" and "Southern History", and records some information that is not included in the official history, so It is often cited by those who later researched the historical events of the Six Dynasties.

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: In the winter of the second year of Baoli (826), Liu Yuxi returned to Luoyang from Hezhou and passed through Jinling.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: The first half of the poem describes the scenery seen and points out the scenic spots and historic sites in Jinling related to the Six Dynasties to hint at the reasons for the rise and fall of the ages; the second half uses discussion and emotion to use the past to satirize the present, revealing the main theme of the whole poem.
works.\u76f8\u5173\u4f5c\u54c1: "Complete Tang Poems"
works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587: 潮满冶城渚，日斜征虏亭。蔡洲新草绿，幕府旧烟青。兴废由人事，山川空地形。后庭花一曲，幽怨不堪听。

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: At the end of the late Tang Dynasty, the politics were dark and the official career was bumpy, and the poet tended to be reclusive.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: Describe the emptiness of Qixia Temple and praise the sense of seclusion.
works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587:
不见明居士，空山但寂寥。
白莲吟次缺，青霭坐来销。
泉冷无三伏，松枯有六朝。
何时石上月，相对论逍遥。
林香雨气新，山寺绿无尘。遂结云外侣，共游天上春。鹤鸣金阁丽，僧语竹房邻。待月水流急，惜花风起频。何方非坏境，此地有归人。回首空门外，皤然一幻身。
明徵君旧宅，陈后主题诗。迹在人亡处，山空月满时。宝瓶无破响，道树有低枝。已是伤离客，仍逢靳尚祠。

works.\u4f5c\u54c1\u540d: 《摄山栖霞寺明征君之碑铭辞》

works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587: 南山势回合，灵境依此住。殿转云崖阴，僧探石泉度。龙蛇争翕习，神鬼皆密护。万壑奔道场，群峰向双树。天花飞不著，水月白成路。今日观身我，归心复何处。

works.\u4f5c\u54c1\u540d: 《与沈十九拾遗同游栖霞寺上方于亮上人院会宿》
works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587:
其一
摄山标胜绝，暇日谐想瞩。
萦纡松路深，缭绕云岩曲。
重楼回树杪，古像凿山腹。
人远水木清，地深兰桂馥。
层台耸金碧，绝顶摩净绿。
下界诚可悲，南朝纷在目。
焚香入古殿，待月出深竹。
稍觉天籁清，自伤人世促。
宗雷此相遇，偃放从所欲。
清论松枝低，闲吟茗花熟。
一生如土梗，万虑相桎梏。
永愿事潜师，穷年此栖宿。
其二
偶来人境外，心赏幸随君。
古殿烟霞夕，深山松桂薰。
岩花点寒溜，石磴扫春云。
清净诸天近，喧尘下界分。
名僧康宝月，上客沈休文。
共宿东林夜，清猿彻曙闻。

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: This poem was probably written when Li Bai visited Jinling in the eighth year of Emperor Xuanzong's Tianbao reign (749). At that time, Li Bai had been away from Chang'an for many years, and he was still concerned about the fate of the country and attached to the imperial court, so he used Laolao Pavilion to say goodbye to express his sadness of being unappreciated.
works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587: 金陵劳劳送客堂，蔓草离离生道旁。古情不尽东流水，此地悲风愁白杨。我乘素舸同康乐，朗咏清川飞夜霜。昔闻牛渚吟五章，今来何谢袁家郎。苦竹寒声动秋月，独宿空帘归梦长。
天下伤心处，劳劳送客亭。春风知别苦，不遣柳条青。

One theory is that this poem was written when the author was ordered to "give gold back to the mountain" in the sixth year of Tianbao (747) and was expelled from Chang'an to travel to Jinling in the south. Another theory is that it was written by the author after he was exiled to Yelang and returned after being pardoned. Some people say that this poem was written by Li Bai during his tour. Yellow Crane Tower, and wrote after "There is a view in front of me, but Cui Hao wrote a poem on it", because he wanted to compete with Cui Hao's "Yellow Crane Tower".
works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587: 凤凰台上凤凰游，凤去台空江自流。吴宫花草埋幽径，晋代衣冠成古丘。三山半落青天外，二水中分白鹭洲。总为浮云能蔽日，长安不见使人愁。

晋末英雄起，神器沦荒服。胡月蚀中原，白日升旸谷。
金陵实形胜，关山固重复。巨壑隍北壖，长江堑西隩。
凿山拟嵩华，穿地象伊谷。草昧席罗图，荜路戴黄屋。
一时因地险，五世享天禄。礼乐何煌煌，文章纷郁郁。
多士春林秀，作颂清风穆。出入三百年，朝事几翻覆。
搀抢如云勃，鲸鲵旋自曝。倦闻金鼎移，骤睹灵龟卜。
吁嗟王气尽，坐悲天运倏。天道何茫茫，善淫乃相复。
行路偏衣半，遂亡大梁族。日隐汀洲上，登舻[舟鹿]川陆。
月回吴山树，风闻楚江鹄。因依兰蕙丛，采襭不盈掬。
恃险不种德，兴亡叹数穷。石城几换主，天堑谩连空。御路叠民冢，台基聚牧童。折碑犹有字，多记晋英雄。

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: Du Mu was very concerned about politics in the early days and expressed concern about the Tang Dynasty, which was riddled with holes at that time. He saw the decadence and mediocrity of the ruling group, the self-defense of the vassal towns, and the frequent border troubles. He felt deeply that social crises were everywhere, and the future of the Tang Dynasty was pathetic. This kind of sorrowful thought prompted him to write many poems with practical significance. "Bo Qinhuai" was produced on the basis of this idea.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: "Po Qinhuai" writes both reality and history. It writes history based on reality. The poem describes the scenery he saw while mooring at Qinhuai at night, lamenting the fall of the previous dynasty and the country. It contains a concise and bitter satire on the behavior of the rulers of the late Tang Dynasty who indulged in sex and lived in dreams. There are only four sentences in the whole poem, but it integrates description of scenery, discussion and lyricism, and it is very natural.
works.\u76f8\u5173\u4f5c\u54c1: "Back Garden Flowers"
works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587: 烟笼寒水月笼沙，夜泊秦淮近酒家。商女不知亡国恨，隔江犹唱后庭花。

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: According to Zhan Ang's "Li Bai Poems and Prose Series", this poem was written in the ninth year of Tianbao. At that time, Li Bai was living in Jinling, and his poem goes: "In the fifth month of the month in the west of Jinling, my ancestors were at Baixia Pavilion. If you want to find the top of Lu Peak, you first go around the Han River." Even when Li Bai was about to visit Mount Lu in May, friends from Jinling said goodbye to Baixia. Pavilion, because of this poem.
works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587: 海水昔飞动，三龙纷战争。钟山危波澜，倾侧骇奔鲸。黄旗一扫荡，割壤开吴京。六代更霸王，遗迹见都城。至今秦淮间，礼乐秀群英。地扇邹鲁学，诗腾颜谢名。五月金陵西，祖余白下亭。欲寻庐峰顶，先绕汉水行。香炉紫烟灭，瀑布落太清。若攀星辰去，挥手缅含情。

works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: It satirizes that Emperor Yang of the Sui Dynasty imitated Empress Chen's example of arrogance, extravagance and lewdness, which led to the destruction of the country.
works.\u76f8\u5173\u4f5c\u54c1: "Yushu Backyard Flower"
works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587: 玉树歌阑海云黑，花庭忽作青芜国。秦淮有水水无情，还向金陵漾春色。杨家二世安九重，不御华芝嫌六龙。百幅锦帆风力满，连天展尽金芙蓉。珠翠丁星复明灭，龙头劈浪哀笳发。千里涵空澄水魂，万枝破鼻飘香雪。漏转霞高沧海西，颇黎枕上闻天鸡。鸾弦代雁曲如语，一醉昏昏天下迷。四方倾动烟尘起，犹在浓香梦魂裹。后主荒宫有晓莺，飞来只隔西江水。

works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587: 凤凰台上凤凰游，凤去台空江自流。吴宫花草埋幽径，晋代衣冠成古丘。三山半落青天外，二水中分白鹭洲。总为浮云能蔽日，长安不见使人愁。
朝别朱雀门，暮栖白鹭洲。波光摇海月，星影入城楼。望美金陵宰，如思琼树忧。徒令魂入梦，翻觉夜成秋。绿水解人意，为余西北流。因声玉琴里，荡漾寄君愁。

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: This poem was probably written around the time of the Anshi Rebellion in Tianbao's last years. Jue Ren associates the crisis of the Tang Dynasty at that time with the Yongjia Rebellion in the Western Jin Dynasty, and associates Xie Andun with the story that Shu'an rose up to save the crisis. The poet lamented Xie An's heroic feat of saving the country in troubled times, and hoped to serve the country like Xie An. In the midst of a national crisis, I realized my long-cherished wish, and thought of my own experience of being unappreciated and full of ambitions, so I wrote this poem to express my beliefs.
works.\u76f8\u5173\u4e8b\u4ef6: Rebellion of Eight Kings
works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587: 晋室昔横溃，永嘉遂南奔。沙尘何茫茫，龙虎斗朝昏。胡马风汉草，天骄蹙中原。哲匠感颓运，云鹏忽飞翻。组练照楚国，旌旗连海门。西秦百万众，戈甲如云屯。投鞭可填江，一扫不足论。皇运有返正，丑虏无遗魂。谈笑遏横流，苍生望斯存。冶城访古迹，犹有谢安墩。凭览周地险，高标绝人喧。想像东山姿，缅怀右军言。梧桐识嘉树，蕙草留芳根。白鹭映春洲，青龙见朝暾。地古云物在，台倾禾黍繁。我来酌清波，于此树名园。功成拂衣去，归入武陵源。
晨登瓦官阁，极眺金陵城。钟山对北户，淮水入南荣。漫漫雨花落，嘈嘈天乐鸣。两廊振法鼓，四角吟风筝。杳出霄汉上，仰攀日月行。山空霸气灭，地古寒阴生。寥廓云海晚，苍茫宫观平。门馀阊阖字，楼识凤凰名。雷作百山动，神扶万栱倾。灵光何足贵，长此镇吴京。

works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: The place sung by "Wuyi Lane" got its name because it was the Wuyi Camp in the Wu Dynasty. After the Jin Dynasty moved eastward, the famous clans lived together for a while. Now, "the swallows are still here, and the kings are scattered, and it has become ordinary." The common people are filled with endless emotions and their pens are extremely curved."

works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: Fuzhou Mountain, now called Jiuhua Mountain, is the first hill on the west side of the Zhongshan Mountains approaching the city. Because the side facing Xuanwu Lake is as steep as a cut, it looks like an overturned boat, so it was called Fuzhou Mountain in ancient times. The author listed in the preface to the poem all the famous places of interest during the Six Dynasties period. The highlight of this poem is the use of the word "capture the boat" as an article that borrows from the past and satirizes the present, comparing the foolish kings of the past dynasties to the same raccoon dog. Fuck the snake god: the mountain god, it is also a witness to history. Xinting: Located in the southwest of Jiankang City, it was a place where people who migrated south during the Eastern Jin Dynasty mourned the memory of the Central Plains. Luobin: refers to Luoyang, also refers to the Central Plains. Jiangzuo: Jiangnan. The Southern Song Dynasty was relatively peaceful, and the dignitaries "wanted to miss Shu". The author reminded the world through the story of "Xinting". Jing Gong: An honorific title given to Wang Anshi because he was granted the title Duke of Jing. Research: The title of the chapter in "The Book of Songs: Wei Feng" sings about the free life among the mountains and rivers, and expresses the praise of the hermit. Tianjin Bridge: In the ancient capital of Luoyang, it is a symbol of history and culture. Passengers on Tianjin Bridge mostly refer to courtiers. The purpose of this article is that Wang Anshi lived in seclusion in Zhongshan in his later years and was still worried about state affairs.

works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: The author cherishes the historical relics and expresses infinite emotion.

works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: The first sentence responds to the title and explains the place and event. The sentence "Thousand Years" leads to the legend of Phoenix, and the second sentence "Tide Return" expresses the desolation of the empty river and the lack of Phoenix. The couplets are neatly contrasted, and the use of words such as color, place names, directions, plants, actions, etc. are strict and sophisticated, depicting a scene of luxuriant river grass, blooming apricot flowers, and full of vitality. The neck couplet pointed out that Fenghuang Terrace has a majestic and dangerous terrain and is a battleground for military strategists. This couplet has both reality and fiction, and it shows that from ancient times to the present, the Phoenix Tower is still there, the drums are wailing, and wars are going on.

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: Li Bai traveled south to Jinling, and Qilu "Climbed the Phoenix Tower of Jinling". In the Northern Song Dynasty, Guo Xiang followed in Taibai's footsteps and also took the stage to compose poems.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: The poet went up to the stage and looked into the distance, feeling the sadness of the illusory human affairs and the allegory and melancholy of the vicissitudes of life and the sudden disappearance of prosperity. This poem has the charm of Taibai's poetry and has its own characteristics, expressing a unique nostalgic feeling.

风摇落日催行棹，湖拥新沙换故洲。
结绮临春无处觅，年年荒草向人愁。

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: When the Southern Song Dynasty collapsed, Jinling's nostalgic poems were more about the pain of death and the sadness of separation.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: In "Touching Fish: Climbing the Phoenix Tower", "Let the snow in the north blur the sky, let the east wind change the green, all the time is spent in dreams and drunkenness", the pain of the country's destruction and the death of the family is heart-breaking.

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: In the fourth year of Chunxi reign of Emperor Xiaozong of the Song Dynasty (1177), the author sent an envoy from Sichuan to summon him back. He set out from Chengdu in May, took the waterway eastward, and arrived in Lin'an (today's Hangzhou, Zhejiang) in October. He wrote this book along with his diary experiences. . The title is taken from the meaning of the sentence "The ship is moored at the door of Eastern Wu Wanli" in Du Fu's poem "The First Four Sentences".
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: "Wu Chuan Lu" recorded his itinerary day by day, with specific and detailed descriptions of every place he passed along the way, including the local monuments, people and geography. Fan Chengda stayed in Jiankang for six days, mainly inspecting places such as the Heart-Appreciating Pavilion, Yulin Hall, Fugui Tower, Qingxi Pavilion, and Liuhe Changlu Temple. He had a thorough understanding of the beautiful scenery and road traffic conditions of these places. The vivid description and beautiful writing not only have literary value, but also have historical value for understanding Nanjing, the capital of the Southern Song Dynasty.

works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: The words are divided into upper and lower parts. The first film is what the poet saw and felt when he came to the scene. "Yao Cen Yuan Mu, offering sorrow and hatred, jade hairpin and snail bun." "Yao Cen Yuan Mu" is a condensation of two sentences in Han Yu's "Chengnan Couplet": "Yao Cen brings out an inch of green, and distant eyes gain double brightness." "The sunset above the building, in the sound of the broken bell, the wanderer in the south of the Yangtze River." If the first three sentences are distant views, then the second sentence "Sunset" is the close-up view: the setting sun hangs on the top of the building and is about to sink; the wild geese that have lost their flock Flying across the sky, the sound of mourning added to the miserable atmosphere. "Looking at Wu Gou, I patted the railings all over, but no one knew it, so I came to the scene." This is written after the protagonist appeared on the scene, and conveyed his rich inner world with two actions full of personality characteristics: He looked at Wu Gou He looked again and again, walked around and patted the railings, lamenting that no one understood his "meaning to come." Li He's "Nan Yuan" said: "Why don't men take Wu Gou and collect the fifty states in Guanshan?" Obviously, the two poems are the origin of the word "Wu Gou" in Xin's poem, which expresses the poet's ambition to regain the lost territory and unify the motherland. Ambition. The second film mainly uses a few allusions to express its ideas, and its ideological context is still connected to the previous film. Because "no one understands it, it means ascending", the poet couldn't help but think of many past events of his predecessors. "Don't say that the perch is worthy of being eaten. When the west wind blows, the eagle has not returned yet?" These lines are quoted from "Shishuo Xinyu Shijian". "Ask for the land and ask for the house, but if you are afraid of being embarrassed to see him, Liu Lang is talented." These lines are alluded to in "Three Kingdoms? Wei Zhi? Chen Deng Biography". "Sorrowful about the storm" refers to the writer's worries about the precarious situation of the country and the increasingly critical current situation. "Trees are like this", quoted from "Shishuoxinyu·Yu". "Qianhe is called, with a red scarf and green sleeves, to find the hero's tears." "Qianhe" actually means that there is no one, so the sentence "no one knows" should be used. The poet is lonely. Not only does no one understand him, but he also has no comforter. In the words, the author places his emotions on the east-flowing river and the boundless autumn light, on the slowly setting sun and the sad sunset. He does not express his sorrow but sees his own sorrow, which makes the emotion and scenery reach a wonderful and rootless state.

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: In the 16th year of the Yuan Dynasty (1279), Deng Yan was captured in Yashan and escorted north together with Wen Tianxiang. Deng Yan wrote this poem while passing through Jiankang.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: This poem contains a deep and affectionate feeling of nostalgic for the past and the present, and the language is extremely bright. It can be called a masterpiece among Deng Shan's existing poems. The first part of the poem reads, "The sparse rain washes the sky, and the pillow mat is cool. A single leaf of the Tung tree makes the sound of autumn." The replacement of solar terms with the retreat of summer and cold illustrates the philosophy of life of prosperity and decline. The Song Dynasty fell, and the homeland was no longer there. It was like a cold autumn, which made people feel discouraged and lazy. "Sparse rain washes the sky and makes it clear." However, the sky is clear and the world is unclear, so what can be done about it. The coolness of the pillows and mats indoors is a realistic representation of the cooler weather and climate change in autumn, and the fallen leaves of the outdoor well trees not only signify autumn, but also evoke the poet's lament about his own life experience. It sets off the atmosphere of the whole poem and contains deep nostalgic emotions. "Who would think that a guest's body is as light as a leaf, floating thousands of miles away?" The leaves drifting in the wind illustrates the uncontrollability of personal destiny, and also shows the author's sadness for the fall of the country. "Thousands of Miles" summarizes the journey from being captured in Guangdong to Jiankang. The next part of the poem reads, "The dream breaks through the ancient city of Taicheng. The moon is pale and the tide is flat." The infinite grief is difficult to resolve. Deng Yan took it to sleep, but when he woke up, he found that the dream in Gutai City was desolate. The poet's mood was already very sad, but when he woke up and saw the dim moonlight and the rising tide, he couldn't help but feel excited, and his heart became even more miserable. After waking up from the dream, he can only go to "visit the new pavilion with wine". At the Xinting meeting that year, Director Wang also advocated "strengthening the royal family and conquering China", but both the poet and Wen Tianxiang were captured, and the Song Dynasty was completely destroyed. "I don't see the Wang Xie's house at that time, and the tobacco is green." Deng Yan's emotion about the impermanence of the world and the rise and fall of dynasties is particularly profound because of his real experience. The conclusion empathizes with the scene, embodying the euphemistic and tragic sound of the country's subjugation. The curls are endless.

These may be the reasons why this poet who writes mostly love and travel poems is so nostalgic.

舣棹秋江十里长，招提结伴且寻将。凭谁寻路蹲狮子，怪尔开门叫凤凰。海外灵槎曾揽胜，岩端健笔自流芳。老僧不解观空旨，对客犹然说郑铛。
其三：姚坊门枣
新秋佳果说姚坊，火齐堆盘喜乍尝。甜到十分知过雨，艳生半颊为迎阳。戏抛恰可同心赤，饱掇何须患齿黄。记得吕家山畔路，薰风先递枣花香。

works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: Bulguksa Temple: This poem retains the historical data about Bulguksa Temple, and the poem also has a Zen meaning. Mengbiyi: Wentong is the calligraphy name of Jiang Yan, a writer of the Southern Dynasties. "Hate Fu" and "Farewell Fu" were all written by him. The idioms "Mengbi produces flowers" and "Jiang Lang's talents are exhausted" are all legends about him. The latter story took place in the post house in Yecheng. Jiang Yan dreamed that an immortal who called himself Guo Pu wanted to take back the colored pen given to him in the early years. From then on, his writing was not as good as before. The author said, "I am not as talented as Jiang, but I need to search for the source." The insight is profound and he hits the nail on the head. Xinxin: Duke Yang Zhongxiang, whose name was Bangyi and whose courtesy name was Xiji, was from Jishui (now Ji'an, Jiangxi Province). In the third year of Jianyan's reign in the Song Dynasty (1129), Jin soldiers invaded Jiankang. At that time, he served as the general magistrate of Jiankang Prefecture. He was captured because Du Chong, who stayed behind, defected. The state refused to surrender. He wrote in blood, "I would rather be a ghost of the Zhao family than a minister of another state." Wanyan Zongbi ordered The man had his heart scraped from his abdomen at the age of 44. In memory of his loyalty to the country, Emperor Gaozong Zhao gave him the posthumous title of "Zhongxiang" and built a tomb, a temple and a stele for him. Today, the memorial site is located on the mountain behind the "Second Spring in the South of the Yangtze River" behind Dong Gang of Yuhuatai. There is also the "Er Zhong Temple" built in Yuhuatai to commemorate him and his fellow countryman Wen Xinguo Gong (Wen Tianxiang). Tangshan: Tangshan is known as "Hot Spring" in ancient times and is named after its hot springs. According to some data, it has been designated as a royal hot spring since the Xiaoliang period of the Southern Dynasties. It has a history of more than 1,500 years and is one of the four major natural hot springs in the country. This poem of five unique poems personifies "hot springs" and says "this mountain has hot intestines". It is a strange imagination and full of fun.
works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587:
佛国寺
在太平门外板仓，古华藏庵。
佛不可思议，莫问寺小大。
国在寺之中，亦在寺之外。
梦笔驿
在冶城。
才如江文通，也惜梦中笔。
我才不如江，但向源头索。
剖心里
在长干里，宋时金人入建康，杨忠襄公邦乂不屈，金人剖腹，取其心。
杨公剖心处，心赤血同赤。
至今墓上草，红心无断绝。
汤山
氤氲气满野，泉水流汤汤。
或者通地肺，此山有热肠。

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: On the eighth day of the eighth lunar month in the tenth year of Yongzheng's reign (1732), the poet took the rural examination at Jiangnan Gongyuan in Nanjing. After the rural examination, he visited Nanjing's scenic spots and historic sites and composed "Niannujiao·Stone City".
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: By describing the decline and desolation of Stone City, the whole poem profoundly expresses the author's helplessness and emotion about the rise and fall of past dynasties.
works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587:
悬岩千尺，借欧刀吴斧，削成城郭。千里金城回不尽，万里洪涛喷薄。王浚楼船，旌麾直指，风利何曾泊。船头列炬，等闲烧断铁索。
而今春去秋来，一江烟雨，万点征鸿掠。叫尽六朝兴废事，叫断孝陵殿阁。山色苍凉，江流悍急，潮打空城脚。数声渔笛，芦花风起作作。

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: Nanjing was the capital of the Six Dynasties, but when the Qing troops went south, Nanjing was severely damaged. When the Ming and Qing dynasties changed, the author happened to visit Yuhuatai. He expressed his sorrow for the Ming Dynasty by describing the desolate and decaying scenery of Jinling City and tracing back to the Six Dynasties. So he wrote this poem and created it specifically. The time is unknown.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: Every word of this poem contains deep emotions about the alternation of rise and fall, and every sentence reveals the sigh of the changes in the world. The whole article has profound and profound artistic conception, and lingering emotions. It can be said that the whole article depicts the scenery as a whole, and it is also lyrical as a whole. The scenery contains deep feelings, and the feelings are expressed in the scenery. The writing is powerful and elegant, using the old sentences of the predecessors, which is natural and harmonious, with no axe marks.

works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587: 衰柳白门湾，潮打城还。小长干接大长干。歌板酒旗零落尽，剩有渔竿。秋草六朝寒，花雨空坛。更无人处一凭栏。燕子斜阳来又去，如此江山。

works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: The characteristic of this poem is that it does not feel desolate because Jinling is no longer the capital, but also affirms Jinling's contribution to the development of southern culture, which is superior to those poems that only focus on the mountains and rivers.

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: Jiming Temple is located on the foothills of Jilong Mountain on the side of Taicheng. It is one of the oldest Buddhist temples in Nanjing. The history can be traced back to the Qixuan Temple in the Eastern Wu Dynasty. The temple was located in the rear garden of the Kingdom of Wu. In the eighth year of the Liang Dynasty in the Southern Dynasties (527), Emperor Liang Wu built Tongtai Temple in Jimingdai, and it has since become a holy place for Buddhism. In the 20th year of Hongwu in the Ming Dynasty (1387), Zhu Yuanzhang rebuilt it and inscribed it as "Jiming Temple".
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: In the five-character ancient poem "Jiming Temple", Zhang Zhidong praised the extraordinary sight of the "landscape, city and forest" integrated from far and near on the Jiming Temple Mountain, and criticized "the white gate to wander around, dragging and lifeless" "I am fascinated by Qin Huainan and will not step on the back of Zhongshan Mountain", which expresses the ambition and courage of a famous official and reformist leader in the late Qing Dynasty to work hard and be enterprising. At the end of the poem, Du Fu's poem is quoted, once again implicitly recording that unforgettable and inspiring moment in the stormy late Qing Dynasty when the cock crows endlessly.
works.\u76f8\u5173\u4f5c\u54c1: "Guangyatang Collection"
works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587:
雨暗覆舟山，泉响鸡鸣埭，
埭流北湖水，僧住南朝寺。
当时造宫城，选此陵阿地,
朝市皆下临，江山充环卫。
白门游冶子，沓拖无生气，
心醉秦淮南，不踏钟山背。
一朝辟僧楼，雄秀发其秘，
城外湖皓白，湖外山苍翠。
南岸山如马，饮江驻鞍辔,
北岸山如屏，萦青与天际，
鹭洲沙出没，浦口塔标识。
烟中万楼台，渺若蚁蛭细，
亦有杜老忧，今朝豁蒙蔽。

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: This poem was written three years after the signing of the Treaty of Nanjing. The author passed by Nanjing, thinking about the past and looking back at the present, worrying about the fate of the country, feeling emotional about it, and writing a poem to express his feelings.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: The poem starts with the historical status and topography of Nanjing. Cui Wei describes the towering city wall. The second couplet of the poem is a commentary on the Nanjing Peace Treaty. Then he switched from actual description to description of scenery. The epilogue pushes the emotional waves of sadness and indignation to a climax.
works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587: 崔巍雉堞尚前朝，形胜东南第一标。惊见羽书传昨夜，忽闻和议出崇朝。秦淮花柳添憔悴，玄武旌旗空寂寥。往事何人更愤切，不堪呜咽独江潮。

works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: The author chose a specific place, time and angle to observe the overall picture and scenic spots, history and reality of Xuanwu Lake. The structure of this poem is "present, ancient, and present": the first and last couplets are "present", and the two middle couplets are "ancient". From the present to the ancient, and from the ancient to the present, people are immersed in the long historical time and space. , and inspires readers to think about the "rise and fall" of history and reality. The smoke, water, sunset, and fishermen's songs in the epilogue have a broad realm and long-lasting emotions, which are quite extraordinary.

works.\u76f8\u5173\u4e8b\u4ef6: Wu Weiye visited Xuanwu Lake
works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587: 覆舟西望接陂陀，千顷澄潭长绿莎。六代楼船供士女，百年版籍重山河。平川岂习昆明战？禁地须通太液波。烟水不关兴废感，夕阳闻已唱渔歌。
关势接苍穹，登临四望空。钟声何处寺，帆影大江风。薄雾山腰白，斜阳鸟背红。此身何处着，端在画图中。

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: In the third year of Shunzhi, Mao Qiling joined Mao Youlun's anti-Qing rebels. Because he opposed Mao Youlun's work with Ma Shiying and Fang Guoan, he denounced Ma and Fang as national traitors. They were the grievances of Fang and wanted to kill them many times. Mao Qiling changed his name and surname, fled to avoid revenge, and lived in the Jianghuai River.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: Describe the ruins of the Ming Dynasty and express the thoughts of the motherland.
works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587: 建业重关控上游，龙盘虎踞旧神州。荆门西扼江流下，越徼东连海气浮。故国山河天堑合，夕阳宫阙石城秋。登临转觉乡关远，日暮苍茫涕泗流。

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: There are many scenic spots in Jinling, but Mei Zengliang and his entourage were particularly fond of it, and they visited the little-known Xiaopan Valley with great interest. This trip was unusual, and the "Travel to Xiaopangu" written about it can be regarded as a small wonder.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: The first sentence at the beginning seems simple, but in fact it is closely related to the whole text. It is the general direction and clue of the author's "seeking Xiaopangu". One word "Qiu" governs the whole article, and the whole article is centered on this. The article goes from hope to hopelessness to hope and then to disappointment, with many twists and turns. The author couldn't find it the first time, so he went to the vicinity of Guiyuntang. The second time he couldn't find it, he went to Lulong Mountain for the third time. The article advances layer by layer with twists and turns. The author's name is Jiyou, but he actually writes about traveling for the sake of seeking. In the process of seeking but not getting, he cleverly and naturally demonstrates the unique charm of Jinghewen. Xiaopangu is everywhere, but looking back, it is Xiaopangu. It seems real yet fictitious, seems fictitious yet real, creates suspense, and ends with doubt. The idea is unique and bold.

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: Nanjing was the capital of the Six Dynasties, but when the Qing troops went south, Nanjing was severely damaged. When the Ming and Qing dynasties changed, the author happened to visit Yuhuatai. He expressed his sorrow for the Ming Dynasty by describing the desolate and decaying scenery of Jinling City and tracing back to the Six Dynasties. So he wrote this poem and created it specifically. The time is unknown.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: Every word of this poem contains deep emotions about the alternation of rise and fall, and every sentence reveals the sigh of the changes in the world. The whole article has profound and profound artistic conception, and lingering emotions. It can be said that the whole article depicts the scenery as a whole, and it is also lyrical as a whole. The scenery contains deep feelings, and the feelings are expressed in the scenery. The writing is powerful and elegant, using the old sentences of the predecessors, which is natural and harmonious, with no axe marks.

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: Among the feudal rulers of China's past dynasties, there were many emperors who loved to recite poems and compose poems, dance and write ink, and admire themselves for being romantic. The famous Emperors Kangxi and Qianlong of the Qing Dynasty loved reciting poems and inscriptions throughout their lives, and they were considered to be the best among them. Out of his love for the mountains and rivers of the south of the Yangtze River and the need to win over the Han intellectuals in the south, Kangxi visited Nanjing many times and wrote poems such as "Nostalgia for the Old Purple Gold City in Jinling". During the reign of Qianlong, he visited the south of the Yangtze River six times and wrote poems and calligraphy wherever he went. Most of his poems are mediocre, but occasionally they are readable. This poem "Inscribed on Yanziji" was written by Hongli during his southern tour to Nanjing in 1715. Afterwards, it was built into a stone tablet and is still on Yanziji today.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: beautiful landscape poem can usually be called a beautiful landscape painting. This poem uses the first two sentences to describe the extraordinary momentum of Yanziji flying in the sky. If it stopped there, the picture would be a bit monotonous. So the last two sentences shift the vision to Baguazhou opposite Yanziji. What appears in the eyes of the author is an endless wilderness transformed by thousands of years of accumulated sand and gravel: green crops, rising smoke, and hard-working villagers. How quiet and peaceful it is. Qianlong was not the founding emperor. What he needed was to protect the country built by his ancestors. Therefore, in his eyes, "Yancun plowing and chiseling in peace for a long time" became the most admirable sight. With the tranquility in the second half, it became even more apparent. The majesty of Yanziji hitting the waves also highlights the nobility of Yanziji's independence despite the vicissitudes of life.

works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587: 片石撼江皋，水激矶头影动摇。阅尽兴亡千古事，萧萧，往日英雄不可招。一剑倚天高，恐有蛟龙起怒涛。铁锁都应拦不住，滔滔，和雨和风卷六朝。

works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: The first part of this poem is written from the upper reaches of the Yangtze River to the lower reaches. "Far and near mountains, jagged palaces" first describes the scenery in sight, and the three sentences "Sigh for the heroes of the Three Chus" contain the meaning of paying homage to the past; the second part is a continuation of the previous part. Coming from ancient times, it first quoted allusions such as the Yangtze River Cairn, Yushu Song, Golden Lotus Dance, and Phoenix Terrace, all of which were related to the rise and fall of the Six Dynasties. Finally, it ended with the phrase "The fisherman's boat went to dusk in the morning" to express the author's broad-minded and tranquil mood. .
works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587: 岷峨万里，见渺渺、水流东去。指远近关山，参差宫阙，起灭长空烟雾。南望沧溟天边影，辨不出、微茫尽处。叹三楚英雄，六朝王霸，消沉无数。从古长江天堑，飞艎难渡。自玉树歌残，金莲舞罢，倏忽飞乌走兔。燕子堂前，凤凰台畔，冷落丹枫白露。但坐看、狎鸥随浪，渔父扁舟朝暮。

works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587: 人天八万四千塔，谁其造者阿育王。江南经始康僧会，舍利爪发传萧梁。永乐制九级，海藏同辉光。天风扶我一登眺，长干万户连千樯。岷峨之脉气葱郁，四面屏障森堵墙。大江一线逝东北，前淮后湖环阴阳。虎龙蟠踞虽得地，英雄割裂无完疆。岂若隋唐大一统，直以弹丸当荆扬。如来涅槃矧千劫，灵物薶瘗诚荒唐。黄旗紫盖俱已矣，空余金地供翱翔。安得神僧捧刹入云去，无令承平士女悲沧桑!

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: During the long journey of thousands of miles, the poet temporarily anchored his boat under the Stone City, overlooking Jinling in the distance, and condensed what he saw, heard and thought into this poem.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: The first couplet narrates the story of "stopping" outside Jinling City. Judging from the title, the boat parking location is on the riverside in the west of the city. Facing the ancient stone city, he longed for it but could not reach it. Because we still need to go on an expedition and are "thousands of miles away from our hearts", there is nothing we can do. I had no choice but to look eastward to appreciate the scenery of Jinling. "Autumn Momo" and "Night Xiao Xiao" have a sad color, which contains the sorrow of traveling and the emotion caused by the ancient stone city. Next, he wrote about the river in front of him, with surging waves extending to the south of Qinhuai. The Baoen Temple Tower in the south of the city stood majestically. The poet traced back to the Six Dynasties from the contemporary Baoen Temple Tower, but he did not describe the ruins of the Six Dynasties in detail, but directly wrote about the decline of the Six Dynasties. In these two poems, the river and the solitary tower form a wonderful horizontal and vertical picture, "Thousands of peaks hang down the six dynasties." There is a contrast between the eternity of nature and the vicissitudes of human affairs, which is quite exquisite. The last two sentences follow the above, "The flowers in the backyard of a businesswoman "Music" is adapted from Du Mu's "Po Qinhuai": "Business girls don't know the hatred of the country's subjugation, but they still sing the flowers in the backyard across the river." The poetic meaning points out the reason for the decline of the Six Dynasties, which is that the ruling class is seeking pleasure and immersed in the decadent life of wealth and wealth. .
works.\u76f8\u5173\u4f5c\u54c1: "Parking at Qinhuai"
works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587: 秣陵城外暂停桡，无奈离心万里遥。极浦蒹葭秋漠漠，隔淮烟雨夜箫萧。大江迭浪浮孤塔，建业千峰吊六朝。商女后庭花一曲，歌声哀怨旅魂销。
石头城上翠孱颜，虎踞龙盘在此间。形胜旧传三国志，风云长护六朝山。登高感慨谁知己，揽辔澄清亦等闲。天遣艰难走西蜀，峨嵋万里隔秦关。

works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: This is a narrative poem. It is the writer pouring out his heartfelt feelings to his friends, and the "small preface" before the words is very clear in describing the story. Friends went boating on Nanhu Lake without asking the author to go with them, so feelings of resentment and admiration arose spontaneously. The author talks in the first person, which is kind and gentle. However, the author does not describe it in a straightforward manner. He still pays attention to the hierarchical structure and expresses an event in daily life with ups and downs. The use of spoken language is varied, sometimes ridiculing, and very distinctive. More importantly, these are used as a foil to remind people of the beauty of Nanhu Lake and the joy of chanting and appreciation. This kind of virtual writing method reflects the author's ingenuity.
works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587: 幼霞、鹤巢、诗孙同泛南湖,联吟买醉,未尝召我也。事后更以词夸我,岂复可恕。当待秋中月满花香,更共觞我,倚此檄之。旧游如梦南湖路，华闲也会舣棹。桂楫偕游，松醪共醉，君悲今番吟眺。如何忘了。有善饭廉颇，也堪同调。一榻清风，恁般偏我已堪恼。相夸还更示我，把联吟秀句，取次赍到。字里华香，行闲酒气，险把衰翁熏倒。秋光正好。趁月晓风清，更偕华笑。补过心虔，买舟须要早。
青溪水木最清华，王谢乌衣六代夸。不奈更寻江总宅，寒烟已失段侯家。

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: "Zhongshan Plum Blossoms" symbolizes the founding of the Zhu Ming Dynasty, and this is also the purpose of "Zhongshan Plum Blossoms" written by Xu Yuanquan after the Yi Dynasty.
A work of art.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: Although the author once served as the chief envoy of Jiangning, he was busy with political affairs and had no time to travel. He had a feeling of "taking responsibility for nothing" towards the scenery of Qixia.
works.\u8bd7\u6b4c\u5168\u6587: 布帆频挂秣陵秋，六代云山瞥眼收。独有栖霞松外路，几回芒屐负清游。

works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: It describes the plunder of the Qing soldiers going south in the early Qing Dynasty, and the decline of Nanjing, which was once the founding capital of the Ming Dynasty, under the iron heel.

征南十万卒，如蚁泊归舟。
悬旆缠云脚，悲笳裂石头。
清平他日梦，行旅夕阳愁。
亡国恨无尽，滔滔江水流。
其二
矶上谁长啸，苍然老匹夫。
江山六朝在，天水一亭孤。
秃树翔归鹭，层涛捲乱凫。
渔舟安稳甚，吹笛入菰蒲。
其三
钟阜云何似，吴陵客重来。
松杉焚已尽，鹳鹤暮空回。
特特高原立，频频倦眼开。
东风吹不歇，草色出寒灰。
其四
即以山为郭，坚完世所稀。
云鸿应得度，塞马竟如归。
陇雨耕时大，人烟战后微。
年年禾与黍，养得骆驼肥。
其五
江路倏昏黑，狂风吹倒人。
怒潮奔上岸，小艇泊无邻。
何事歌弹铗，惟应把钓缗。
深深建业水，欲饮转伤神。
其六
兵火犹存寺，乾坤未息戈。
前朝寄何物，古树指娑罗。
阴冷山僧坐，花香水鸟多。
盘桓吾不厌，一日几回过。
其七
鼓鼙声飒飒，道路色凄凄。
盘髻妇驰马，横刀兵捉鸡。
山城常罢市，帝里已成畦。
黄屋光辉瓦，纷纷碎入泥。
其八
寒潮看又落，渐渐见山根。
拾蚌涧沙软，打鱼江水浑。
饥民春满路，米店昼关门。
吾亦糊吾口，愁来只自言。
其九
短褐张道士（张瑶星。），长安旧锦衣。
饥眠紫峰阁，老掩白云扉。
尘世鹿还逐，鼎湖龙不归。
时时一回首，血泪尽情挥。
其十
高情忆吴季，小筑面秦淮。
书帙集连屋，兰花开上阶。
看人浇菜圃，邀我到茆斋。
山色在门外，春风多好怀。
高台多春风，旭日照彼岨。揽袂试登临，怀古遥踟蹰。昔者周孝侯，奋身三恶除。家本罨画溪，折节此读书。古今同一辙，与君皆侨居。工愁吴季重，深情王伯舆。抗志慕贤达，悠悠千载馀。

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: This is a poem about things, and it is also a political lyric poem. In the poem, the swallow is shaped into an artistic image that is permeated with the author's thoughts and feelings, and the swallow's living habits and activity patterns are vividly described, and the poet's self is everywhere between the lines. It can be said that swallows are written everywhere, and every sentence is a metaphor for people and events; writing about swallows can conform to the characteristics of swallows, and the stories can reveal the meaning of things.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: This is a long seven-character poem in ancient style. The poem integrates narrative and lyricism. It adopts the technique of personification and expresses feelings by chanting about swallows. By describing the sentimentality and attachment of swallows to the Forbidden City, it expresses the poet's deep thoughts about his motherland. A sad feeling of rise and fall.

君不见故宫燕，春雨秋风几回转。
宫中风雨长蓬蒿，飞入宫墙绕虚殿。
穿帘度阁羽差池，尽日呢喃人未知。
柳绿乍衔千尺絮，花开仍拂万年枝。
玉阶寂寞罘罳冷，画栋差池春燕影。
岁岁营巢竟不成，春来秋去谁曾省。
可怜此度秋风早，整顿毛衣犹自好。
裴徊欲别未央宫，万户千门忽如扫。
钟虡何年去洛阳，仙人辞汉泪成行。
最苦西飞双燕子，回头不见旧宫墙。
其二
鸟啼金井桐，啼声易呜咽。
未若向西风，伤心故宫燕。
白门燕子去秋风，社日年年无不同。
惟有曾栖青璅闼，欲去未去悲西宫。
却忆西宫前岁筑，禁篽勾陈俄仿佛。
不道君王事卧薪，颇闻将作夸神木。
一月宫前才列仗，翠华西去尘飘飏。
虚殿无人燕子来，三春对语雕梁上。
转眼谁知事更非，雕梁藻井似蓬飞。
明岁坏垣春草里，茫茫何处问乌衣。

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: In the 20th year of Zhizheng of the Yuan Dynasty (1360), the author, Song Lian, Zhang Yi and Ye Chen went to Jinling at the call of Zhu Yuanzhang. The author climbed Zhongshan Mountain and saw the peach blossoms blooming in October, so he wrote this poem.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: The first part of the work focuses on the word "peach blossom", which expresses the noble and extraordinary identity and graceful and refined form of peach blossom. The second piece closely follows the seasonal environment of "October" and expresses the loneliness and desolation of "Looking back and sharing the joy." It seems that all the beauty is given to the remains of weeds and smoke. The poet's feelings about the vicissitudes of the Six Dynasties permeate between the lines.

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: After Emperor Taizu Zhu Yuanzhang of the Ming Dynasty unified the world, he built Yuejiang Tower on the top of Lion Mountain in Nanjing. He personally wrote a "Record of Yuejiang Tower" and then ordered each of the civil servants to write one. So the author wrote this "Yuejiang Tower".
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: "The Story of Yuejiang Tower" is a eulogizing prose written by Song Lian, a writer in the Ming Dynasty. In the article, the author writes about the beautiful scenery of Yuejiang Tower in the capital (now Nanjing), which is intended to embellish the prosperous times and praise the virtues of the Ming Dynasty. At the same time, the author also hopes that the emperor can appease both internal and external affairs, be considerate of people's livelihood, and incorporate the idea of loyalty to the emperor and concern for the people. The full text is both narrative and argumentative, both parallel and prose, which further demonstrates the author's extraordinary skill.

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: Yuhuatai is located in the south of Jinling City. According to legend, Master Yunguang gave lectures here during the reign of Emperor Wu of Liang Dynasty, which moved the gods and caused flowers to fall like rain. "The Lotus Sutra" says: "The Buddha taught the Dharma that mandala flowers rained down from the sky, so it was called the Rain Flower Terrace." This poem expresses the endless emotions of the past and present and the embrace of sorrow for the country through the description of the sunset scenery of the Rain Flower Terrace.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: The first couplet starts from the legend of Yuhuatai. There should have been a rain of flowers on the Yuhuatai, but when I came today, there was no rain of flowers to be found, only ancient moss everywhere. Comparing these two scenes, without any explanation, the rise and fall of Yuhuatai can be clearly seen, and the poet's sense of the past and present is also implicit in it. The couplet continues to mourn the past and hurt the present. "Qingfan" originally refers to the sound of monks chanting sutras. Here it means that the sound of Master Yunguang's lectures can no longer be heard. The sentence "The chaotic mountains still hold cups against each other" implicitly uses two Buddhist scriptures. One is that "The Biography of Gao Xian of Lotus Society" says that Master Daosheng came to Huqiu Mountain, gathered stones as disciples, and taught the "Nirvana Sutra", and all the stones nodded. The second one is found in the "Biography of Eminent Monks": "The cup-holder, whose name is unknown, often crosses the river in a wooden cup, because of his name." If you do not cultivate carefully and do not practice diligently, you will drink alcohol and eat meat, just like ordinary people. "The word "holding the cup" in the poem refers to this. Combining these two sentences, it means that the eminent monk like Yun Guang whose sermons can make Tianyuhua no longer exists, so these stubborn rocks in the mountains can only face each other. Laity monks like Beidu are still a pile of lifeless rocks in the mountains, expressing their great regret that Yuhuatai is not what it used to be. And lamenting Yuhuatai actually reflects the declining situation of the Ming Dynasty. From the description of the scene below It can be seen more clearly in the middle. The neck couplet describes the scenery seen from a distance. "Crawling grass" means creeping grass. "Crawling grass goes to the horizon" means that the creeping grass of Yuhuatai extends to far away places, implying that desolation like Yuhuatai is everywhere. The scene. The poet added the word "Six Dynasties" before "Mancao", and the metaphor became clearer. The Six Dynasties were all short-term dynasties that established their capitals in Jinling and perished one after another. Needless to say, the poet was worried about state affairs. "Wanli Yangtze River" "Sitting down", Yuhuatai was originally a platform dune formed by the sedimentation of the ancient Yangtze River. It is still very close to the Yangtze River, so you can naturally see it from the stage. "Sitting" refers to the place where Master Yunguang originally lectured, that is Refers to the Yuhuatai. "The Yangtze River of thousands of miles has come down", reading it seems to feel the momentum of the Yangtze River rolling in, shaking the Yuhuatai. It is like Du Fu's "I suspect it is coming from Kongtong, fearing that it will touch the sky pillar and break" in "From Beijing to Fengxian County" The worries of "Yong Huai Five Hundred Words". These two lines have a desolate and majestic state, and the sigh is far away. The last couplet is two lines, and the upper neck couplet writes the dusk scene of Changqian to express the sigh of current events. Changqian, under the rain flower terrace. "Wu Du Fu" "Note on "Changgan Continuation": "There are hills (i.e. Yuhuatai) five miles south of Jianye, with flat land in between, where officials and people live together. There are big and small Changgan in the Changgan, all connected. "Jiagu" is a musical instrument used in the ancient army to record time. During the reign of Emperor Wuzong of the Ming Dynasty, Ning Wang Chenhao rebelled against Nanchang and was captured. After that, Jinling and other places strengthened their military preparedness, so there was the sound of Jiagu in Changqian at dusk. The sentence starts from the distance and looks back at the near distance. The first sentence uses the sound of "笳 drum" to cover the desolate atmosphere and hints at the emergency of the military situation; the second sentence uses the bright lights of the "tower" to describe the drunken life, dreams and death of the nobles. The contrast is sharp and ironic. strong.

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: This poem was written in the second year of Hongwu (1369), the first year of the Ming Dynasty, not long after the founding of the Ming Dynasty. The author was born in the late Yuan Dynasty and early Ming Dynasty, and suffered from war and chaos. At that time, the poet was drafted to participate in the compilation of "History of the Yuan Dynasty". He had ideals and wanted to do something for the country. When he climbed up to the Rain Flower Terrace in Jinling and looked at the Yangtze River shrouded in smoke and sunset, he followed the ups and downs of the river's waves, his thoughts and feelings, and he composed the poem with emotion.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: "Climbing the Rain Flower Terrace in Jinling and Looking at the River" is a seven-character ancient poem written by Gao Qi, a poet of the Ming Dynasty. This poem is a hymn for the reunification of the motherland. The author climbed to the Rain Flower Terrace in Jinling and looked at the Yangtze River rolling eastward. He couldn't help but be moved by the scene, remembering the past and thinking about the present. He wrote this poem to express his yearning for the unification of the country and the peace of the world. The first eight sentences describe the scenery seen when looking from the platform, the next four sentences describe one's nostalgia for the past, the next eight sentences describe the many wars that took place in this place from the Three Kingdoms to the Six Dynasties, and the last four sentences praise. Zhu Yuanzhang's new dynasty and ended with a prayer for peace. The whole poem has strong writing power, sonorous rhyme, easy scrolling and vertical and horizontal momentum.

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: Under the title of the original poem, there is a small preface: "At that time, I was called to suppress the left soldiers." In the spring of the first year of Hongguang (1645), the Qing army was approaching the Jianghuai River, but the Nanming general Zuo Liangyu led hundreds of thousands of troops from Wuhan eastward to attack Nanjing in the name of "Qing Jun side, except Ma Ruan". Ma Shiying, who was in power, ordered Shi Kefa in Jiangbei to withdraw his troops to prevent Zuo Liangyu. Shi Kefa had to send reinforcements to Yanziji, leaving Huaihe defense empty. Later, Zuo Liangyu was defeated by Huang Degong and died of vomiting blood. His son Zuo Mengeng led his tribe to surrender to the Qing Dynasty. Shi Kefa was ordered to return north. At this time, Xuyi surrendered to the Qing Dynasty and the city of Sizhou fell. Shi Kefa then went to Yangzhou and fought desperately to resist the Qing army's attack. Judging from this historical fact, "Yanzijikou Zhan" should have been written when Shi Kefa's "human aid" arrived in Nanjing at Yanziji.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: The military situation is extremely urgent, and Nanming is troubled internally and externally. The whole family was in the city, and Shi Kefa, who was so close that he could not return, climbed to Yanzijitou. Faced with the critical situation of war, he leaned over the river and sighed with emotion. This five-character quatrain improvised by Wanduan not only expresses his feelings for The deep thoughts of his mother and family reflect his deep sense of justice and courage to devote himself to the country at a critical moment. It is said that "men don't shed tears easily", but the line in the poem "the clear tears shed on the sandstone, and the drops sink to the bottom of the river" is so shocking!

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: "A Journey to Zhongshan" is a prose written by Song Lian in 1361. At that time, the author was hired by Zhu Yuanzhang, the Taizu of the Ming Dynasty, and participated in Zhu Yuanzhang's shogunate with Liu Ji and others. He had the opportunity to come to Zhongshan and wrote this travelogue.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: Zhongshan, with its precipitous terrain and beautiful scenery, has attracted literati of all ages to write about it. It is easy to repeat the same scenery when writing about Zhongshan. Song Lian, on the other hand, captured the scenery with his delicate brushstrokes, and relied on his strong painting language ability to describe the sounds, colors, and movements of the scenery, achieving the artistic state of "seeing the words and seeing the appearance", and achieved the effect of "conquering more with less."
It describes the mystery and solemnity of the Ming Xiaoling Mausoleum, the mausoleum of Zhu Yuanzhang, the founder of the Ming Dynasty.

works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: This is a piece of mourning. There is a small preface before the poem: "Han Gao is like an imperial ancestor, and he spent his whole life with little writing, so it is like this. It is Mr. Chen who was the supervisor of Riling." What this poem expresses is not just ordinary nostalgia. The feeling is obviously filled with the feeling of injustice that talents are not appreciated and people are down and out. The first four sentences are the author's personal situation, and the last four sentences are the memory of Taizu's career and the emotion of being born at the wrong time. While containing strong dissatisfaction with the indistinguishability of the wise from the foolish in the real society, the poet has high self-expectations. The whole poem is full of melancholy emotions, bright language, and intriguing meaning.

当时事业难身遇，冯仗中官说与听。

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: This poem was written in the 19th year of Hongwu in the Ming Dynasty (1386). The author completed his mission and left Nanjing to return to China. The ship's route should be out of the west gate of the water, enter the Yangtze River, and follow the sea of people.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: Shuiximen faces Bailuzhou in the middle of the river. Those who are familiar with Chinese classical literature can easily think of Li Bai's nostalgic poem "Ascending the Phoenix Terrace of Jinling". While the author was boating on the river, although he did not have the same melancholy as when he was banished to immortals, he could not suppress his admiration for the past philosophers, so he sang in a low voice to pay tribute to the previous sages.

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: Kong Zhigui used the tone of fictional mountains and forests to sharply satirize the so-called hermits who pretended to live in seclusion in the mountains but sincerely yearned for glory and wealth. There is also a theory that it is a joke to ridicule people who pretend to live in seclusion in the mountains.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: The whole article uses personification to describe the humiliating and angry mood of the mountain scenery. The description of the mountains, rivers and vegetation is vivid, the language is refined and beautiful, the dialogues are neat, the phonology is harmonious, and it is full of strong lyrical charm. The author brings out all the mountain and forest scenery, and makes them full of human emotions, such as joy, anger, sorrow, and joy, talking, laughing, cursing, teasing, and ridiculing, pointing the finger at the fake hermit Zhou Yong, thus profoundly exposing the fact that the scholar-bureaucrats are pretending to be noble and noble, but they are actually working hard. The ugly soul of striving for fame and gain has profound anti-judgment and practical significance.

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: In the eighth year of Yongming Dynasty, he followed Wang Xiao Zilong as the general of Zhenxi and the assassin of Jingzhou, and joined the army with Xie Tiao as the meritorious cao of Zhenxi. Xie Tiao therefore went to Jiangling in the spring of the following year. He traveled westward along the Yangtze River from Jiankang (today's Nanjing). He passed through three mountains in the evening, climbed up and looked over, and looked back at the capital. He was moved by the scene and expressed the poet's feeling of nostalgia for his country and his hometown.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: The whole poem is divided into three organic parts. The first two sentences are the first part. Borrowing ancient poems as inspiration to sing your own voice is the so-called "borrowing guests to accompany you" method (Fang Dongshu's language), which is actually a kind of metonymy. The second part describes the scenery. The poet captured the most characteristic image with his unique personal feelings. He also looked at Jingyi, dozens of miles away in Jiankang, which was currently illuminated by the bright sunset. The third part is lyrical. It shows that he has not forgotten his love and still knows how to look back home. Who can help but have gray hair? The whole poem has smooth language, harmonious tones, natural allusions, and exquisite counterpoints, all of which are in line with the characteristics of "Yongming style".

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: The artistic value of Buddhist temple poetry in the Southern Dynasties is not very high. Frankly speaking, most of them are indifferent to deal with the emperor. Praising and flattering Buddhist temples is actually indirectly flattering the emperor, and there is no real feeling and nature. Revealing is, at most, just a show off of talent. But Liu Lingxian is different. In addition to using the Buddhist temple as a background for the characters' activities in a deeper level, she also boldly shows real human feelings without any pretense or concealment.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: It seems that not a single word in this poem is intended to refer to the Buddhist temple, but there is not a single word that does not wish to write about the Buddhist temple. The noisy "long corridor" and "wide hall" are just nothingness that is flaunted on the surface, but the real thing may be when there is "no sound" in the "qufang". The contrast between movement and stillness, the contrast between noise and silence, the contrast between nothingness and reality, not only vividly reflects reality, but also is full of profound philosophy. The scenes written in the poem can indeed give people infinitely rich and obscure imagination space.

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: This poem was probably written in the early winter of the seventh year of Ming Dynasty (463) by Emperor Xiaowu of the Southern Song Dynasty. In the second year of the Ming Dynasty, Bao Zhao was "abandoned and dismissed from office" and wandered north of the Yangtze River. In the sixth year of the Ming Dynasty, he joined the army and joined the army under the command of Liu Zixu, the king of Linhai in Jingzhou (now Jiangling, Hubei Province). This poem was written by Bao Zhao, who came to Beijing for sightseeing as the special envoy of King Linhai, paid a pilgrimage to the emperor, and passed through three mountains. It describes the author's complicated state of mind when he was looking at the country and thinking about his family. Sanshan is a ferry crossing of ancient Jinling. You can board a boat here when leaving the country, or you can abandon the boat and land here when returning to Beijing. But if the author wants to look at Stone City, he has to climb the hillside. The whole poem changes according to rhyme and rhyme, and is roughly divided into three sections: the eight sentences in the first section write "Going to the Three Mountains to Climb and View", the eight sentences in the middle section write "Looking at Stone City", and the last six sentences express the feeling of "returning the capital".
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: The two sentences "source of spring" and "end of Sichuan" start from a distance, and they are summarized in the sentence structure of flowing water. The springs at the source of the Yangtze River are overflowing and the water is stable. The end of the Yangtze River is rushing with big waves and the color of the water is clear. It shows that the author returned to the capital by boat from Jingzhou. , a feeling of smoothness and stability along the way. The two sentences "morning light" and "xiaoqi" intertextualize, using the word "morning dawn" to indicate the moment of "reaching the three mountains", implying that the poet has a long journey, sailing at night in a boat, and sailing to the three mountains west of Kyoto at dawn, only to see The early morning sunshine covers the sparkling river surface where aquatic animals swim, but there is still white mist gathering among the mountains and dense forests. This is the scene when the sun has just risen. The two sentences "two rivers" and "three mountains" mean that if you look to the north, you will see that the bright Yangtze River is divided into two sections, flowing away away. The three mountains below you are densely forested, arranged one by one, standing on the bank of the river. These two sentences describe the scenery of a river and a mountain, one horizontal and one vertical, one bright and one dark, one beautiful and one majestic, with different styles, forming a unique natural landscape reflecting the rivers and mountains to the west of Jinling City. Anyone who leaves Beijing to the west or comes east to return to the capital will first see the "Two Rivers" and "Three Mountains". At this point, the poet broadens his sight again and looks at the busy scenes of "Southern Sail" and "Beibang" on the Yangtze River illuminated by the morning light. He can only see the sails rising in the south, the poles swinging in the north, or "looking at the higher mountains". It connects Jiangsu and Zhejiang, or "refers to Qihe" and connects to Shandong, which highlights Nanjing's important position as the Yangtze River waterway transportation artery. This scene description uses springs, rivers and rivers as the main image, and the writing is swaying, smooth and elastic. Beautiful. The two sentences "Guanzhi" and "jindai" turn into the joy of viewing the country in "Looking at Stone City". The so-called "guanguo" means viewing the scenic spots and majesty of Kyoto. "Guanzhi" means passes and fortresses; "Heaven" "Yi" refers to Jinling City, the imperial capital. Guandu naturally refers to Stone City, but the word "rao" and the word "hold" obviously include the numerous military fortresses around Jinling City, and in the distance there are passes surrounding Tiandu. For example, there is Anqing in the west, Zhenjiang in the east, Xuzhou in the north, Taihu Lake in the south, etc., forming a network defense circle to protect the imperial capital. The "belt" obviously refers to the Yangtze River, Qinhuai River, Xuanwu Lake, Sanshan, Zhongshan, Stone City, Yaxie City, Dongfu City, etc., surround the capital like a belt. One "circle" and one "embrace", either far or near, symbolize the surrounding pass and fortress surrounding Jinling. The whole world of nine states is like stars surrounding the North Pole, expressing the author's political attitude of respecting the imperial capital. The two sentences "Great Wall" use an exaggerated style to praise the danger of the Stone City Pass as being more dangerous than the world-famous Great Wall. In contrast, , "The Great Wall is not steep and dangerous." The Great Wall is not deep and dangerous, but the craggy stone city is steep and dangerous, like thorn branches, long and sharp, and jagged and astonishing. The two sentences "Zan Lou" focus on describing the stone The tall city towers and city walls attract people. Since the time when Sun Quan of the Eastern Wu Dynasty built the city walls and trained the navy, Stone City's importance to Jiankang and the Southern Dynasty's regime has grown day by day. "Stone City with Tigers" used to be people's view of the dangerous situation here. A vivid summary, Bao Zhao even made his own discovery: The stone city is built on three mountains, close to Tianyi, with Beijing in its lap, and the city walls are rugged. Not only does it sit on the river and lie in a ravine, but its uneven heights are enough to create a strange and dangerous situation. You see The city towers are crowded and towering, and the battlements stretch out to reach the clouds. It is enough to defeat the enemy and brave the capital, and defend the country from stubborn invaders. The stone city is the screen guard of the capital. The beautiful scenery makes people happy, the shape is inspiring, and the long journey How can a returning wanderer not be excited? Bao Zhao said: "A conqueror likes to see the country, but a wandering man is late to see home. "What does "chi" mean? "If you want to be fast and think of the other as slow, you are called Chi" ("Zhengyun"). I want to go faster, faster, but always think it is too slow, too slow. This is called "late". The word "chi" vividly expresses the wanderer's eagerness to go home. In short, the two sentences "Zheng Hu" are a link between the previous chapter and the following chapter, using the words "xi" and "chi" to awaken the poet's complex feelings about the country. The middle section contains narratives and discussions. The main image of the scenery is composed of gate towers and battlements. The brushstrokes are majestic and powerful, reflecting the strong beauty of Stone City. "Lianlian" and "误躅" are followed by "viewing the country" and "seeing home" to express feelings. The sentence "Lian Lian" refers to the performance of "Ren Jing Yin", which is the ancient "Enter the Dynasty Song", to express majesty; the sentence "Wandering" refers to singing a song about hometown to express the song of the heart. "Liu Lian" and "Wander around" are a pair of two-tone continuous words, which not only add to the rhythmic beauty of the poem, but also subtly convey the poet's complex psychology after "abandoning his official position" and "resigning from home to join the army". It is worth noting that "wangxiang", which is compared with "renjing", contains rich connotations. The so-called "hometown songs" refer to poems that express the feeling of homesickness. There are many excellent works since ancient times. From the perspective of the poetic connotation, it is probably based on the poetic meaning of "Standing and looking at my hometown, Gu Ying is pitiful and self-pity" from Lu Ji's "Going to Luodaozhong to Compose". The poet wanders alone and sighs about his hometown, implicitly revealing the pain of being frustrated in his official career, melancholy and self-pity. The four lines of "Mi Qian" lament the hard work of returning to the capital. The further we go, the more we lament the rush of time. The closer we get to the imperial capital, the more we feel the fatigue of the long waterway. It's still okay to travel "together with chariot servants" Such a lament about the hardships and dangers of the journey, then what will happen if you are alone on the broad and vast journey? Here the poet expresses the feelings of loneliness at the end of the year, the passing of time, and the difficulty of life's journey, and he expresses the joy of observing the country. The off-key riff creates a frustrated and sad aftertaste.

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: It was written in the spring of the second year of Emperor Jianwu of Qi Ming Dynasty (495) when Xie Zao was on his way to become the prefect. Xinlinpu is located southwest of Jinling, the capital, not far from the capital. It shows that Xie Tiao had just set out on his journey when he wrote this poem.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: At this time, the poet was in a very conflicted mood. Although he was favored by King Sui and Emperor Ming of Qi due to his outstanding literary talent, and his official career was relatively smooth; however, he had suffered slander and tasted the taste of backstabbing. Therefore, the two thoughts of settling for an honorary position and retreating bravely were always entangled in his chest. He always wants to find a compromise between the two. This poem reflects his mentality. The first half describes the scenery seen during the trip to the river, which hints at the feeling of leaving the country and leaving home; the second half describes the thoughts of living in a secluded place far away from harm, which is quite self-comforting. "Having a feeling of happiness and wealth, and restoring the interest of Cangzhou" is exactly what he hopes for.

works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: This poem mainly expresses the feeling of missing people far away. However, it was late and cold in the night, and good dreams were impossible. The second part was about the long distance and heavy news. , the return date is uncertain, and there is no basis for meeting each other. The sound of the anvil in the moonlight at night cannot comfort the lovesickness, but only adds to the pain of lovesickness. The whole word uses expression techniques such as reflection, association, and rendering, and the image is concrete and vivid, full of life.

works.\u521b\u4f5c\u80cc\u666f: In the spring of 1932, Shen Zupen, who was studying in the Chinese Department of the School of Liberal Arts of Central University, wrote her famous early work "Huanxi Sand" in the poetry course taught by Mr. Wang Dong, dean of literature and director of the Chinese Department: "The grass records the wonderful travels every year. The mountains and rivers still open their eyes. The sound of drums and rivets is filled with long thoughts. Who wrote the poem about the orioles in March? One day, the wind blew alone on the building. There is spring sorrow in the setting sun!" Mr. Wang Dong read this word, "For the September 18th Incident in 1931 I was surprised that the post-war national crisis was reflected in such a subtle and profound way in the writings of a young girl, so I asked her to talk and encourage her. From then on, she became more interested in learning ci and became more confident." This is Mr. Cheng Qianfan's account in "The Biography of Shen Zufen". Cheng Wen also recalled that after the outbreak of the Anti-Japanese War in 1937, the suffering of the nation and the hardship of personal displacement caused the female poet to write a series of phrases with deeper connotations. Express the feeling of the rise and fall of family and country.
works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: The three poems of "Huanxisha" were written while teaching at Jinling University and West China University in Chengdu from 1942 to 1946. "In the late period of the Anti-Japanese War, the ugly face of the Kuomintang reactionaries was gradually exposed. The two completely different lives of reveling in wealth and hunger and crying in cold weather made the female poet extremely indignant. She took up her pen to fight. In her poems during this period, she wrote faithfully It showed some aspects of the political and social life at that time. These four years were her most creative period." (Quoted from Cheng Qianfan's "The Biography of Shen Zufen") In the 1960s and 1970s, Tai Jing, a professor at National Taiwan University, a famous writer and calligrapher Mr. Nong once copied the poem "Huanxi Sha" written by the female poet in his own handwriting, and added a note beside the copy: "This was written by Shen Zufen during the Anti-Japanese War. Li Yian was on his way to the south, but he never felt this way." It can be seen from this group of "Huanxi Sha" 》The spread and influence are wide.

works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: Fuzhou Mountain, also known as Xiaojiuhua Mountain, is located on the west side of Taiping Gate in the east of Nanjing City. It is the first hill where the remnants of the Purple Mountain Mountain enter the city from the west. It is separated from the Ming City Wall in the north and adjacent to Xuanwu Lake. There are temples in the mountain and a tower on the top of the mountain. There is a lake beside it and a city at the foot of the mountain.

Tang Tao climbed up Jingshan Mountain on October 18, 1944. Looking south from the central pavilion, what he saw was: "The buildings are decorated with red doors, red bricks and green tiles. They are neat and a little deserted, and it already looks a little bit in decline." ( Tang Tao's "Ten Days in the Imperial City") Beiping in 1944 was still the old capital that fell. Although it was a sunny day when we visited Jingshan, the Forbidden City with its golden glazed tiles still looked "deserted" and "declined" in Tang Tao's eyes, which was different from the "splendid" and "dazzling" that Zhao Qingge praised. The author's concern for the country is scattered in the text.

短衣散发三千里，亡命南来哭孝陵。

works.\u5185\u5bb9\u7b80\u4ecb: Later generations said that my sister-in-law was the third sister of Jiang Ziwen, the Moling captain in the late Han Dynasty. Ziwen was injured and died during the battle. Wu Sunquan once built a temple for him in Zhongshan, and my sister-in-law was also worshiped as a god. Qingxi originates from Zhongshan Mountain. There is a saying in the song "where my sister-in-law lives, she is alone without a husband", which is the origin of the idiom "my sister-in-law lives alone" in later generations. The story takes place near Sixiang Bridge in Xianbaixia District.
